---
title: "DEEP-SEAM independent replication and four-region transferability test"
subtitle: "Does the published deep-anomaly-detection advantage hold beyond the dataset it was tuned on?"
date: 2026-05-11
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-fold: true
---

## The experiment

DEEP-SEAM (Luo et al. 2026, *Geoscientific Model Development*) is a
recent deep-anomaly-detection architecture for mineral prospectivity
that reported beating Random Forest by 2 to 3 times at top-k capture
rates on a Curnamona Province REE dataset containing seven known
deposits. The architecture uses a small neural network trained with
a "deviation loss" that pushes predicted scores for positive cells
far above a Gaussian reference distribution drawn fresh in each
training batch. The network is told to find what looks unusual
compared to the bulk of the data.

We reimplemented the architecture in PyTorch from the published
methods sections (Pang et al. 2019 for the core deviation network plus
DEEP-SEAM's published preprocessing pipeline) without consulting the
authors' code, then ran a 2-by-2 controlled experiment across four
regions:

| Region | Commodity | Positives | Tectonic setting |
|---|---|---|---|
| Curnamona, South Australia | Rare earth elements | 7 | Single Proterozoic IOCG-style province |
| Tanacross, Alaska | Porphyry Cu-Mo | 45 | Mixed metallogenic history (100+ Myr) |
| Southeast Arizona | Porphyry Cu | 191 (MRDS), 7 (USGS GCA-curated) | Single Laramide event (66-74 Ma) |
| Lawley 2022 continental MVT | Sediment-hosted Zn-Pb | 2,027 | Two continents (US/Canada + Australia) |

For each region, a controlled four-cell experiment compared:

|   | DEEP-SEAM's method (DevNet) | Random Forest |
|---|---|---|
| **DEEP-SEAM's data style** (PCA-decomposed geochemistry, GLCM-textured geophysics) | Cell A | Cell B |
| **Our data style** (raw public open data, no decomposition) | Cell C | Cell D |

The goal was to test whether DEEP-SEAM's architectural advantage
transfers from its tuning dataset to standard regional MPM workflows
on public open data.

## How DevNet consumes the data

DevNet trains a small multi-layer perceptron (input → 24 → 12 → 1
ReLU activations) with a deviation loss:

$$L(x, y; \theta, \mu_R, \sigma_R) = (1 - y) \cdot |dev(x)| + y \cdot \max(0, a - dev(x))$$

where $dev(x) = (\phi(x; \theta) - \mu_R) / \sigma_R$, $\mu_R$ and
$\sigma_R$ are mean and std of a reference sample drawn from a
standard normal distribution at each batch, and $a$ is a confidence
margin. The loss pushes positive cells (y = 1) to have scores far
above the Gaussian reference (anomalies in the upper tail), and
unlabeled cells (y = 0) to have scores near the reference mean.
The implicit assumption is that prospective cells are *anomalies in
feature space* — points that look very different from the bulk of
the data.

## Top-1% capture rate, the four-region comparison

The headline metric for an exploration-targeting model is what
fraction of held-out deposits land in the top 1 percent of cells
the model ranks most prospective. Random sampling gives 1 percent
capture; useful models should give 10× to 50× that. Here are the
numbers across the four regions:

```{python}
#| label: cross-region-table
#| code-fold: true
import json
from pathlib import Path
import pandas as pd

# Lawley Phase 3 has the head-to-head; the within-region numbers
# (Curnamona / Tanacross / Arizona) come from the cross-region
# experiment writeup at research/cross_region_methodology_findings_2026-05.md.
rows = [
    ("Curnamona REE", "rare earth", "7", "0%", "43%", "DevNet wins (reproduces published)"),
    ("Tanacross porphyry Cu-Mo", "Cu-Mo", "45", "22.2%", "13.3%", "RF wins"),
    ("Arizona porphyry Cu", "Cu", "191", "23.0%", "8.9%", "RF wins"),
    ("Lawley continental MVT (2-D blocked)", "Zn-Pb", "2027", "28.4%", "0.0%", "GBM wins"),
]
df = pd.DataFrame(rows, columns=[
    "Region", "Commodity", "Positives", "RF/GBM top-1% capture",
    "DevNet top-1% capture", "Result",
])
df
```

DevNet wins on Curnamona (replicating the published 2-3× advantage)
and loses by 9-28 percentage points of top-1% capture on every other
region. The 2,027-positive Lawley benchmark is the largest test we
have, and it produces the cleanest result: under 2-D spatial-block
CV, the gradient-boosted-trees model captures **28% of held-out
deposits in its top 1% of cells**, while DevNet captures **0% of
deposits in its top 1%**. DevNet's predictions on Australia (trained
on US/Canada) score AUC 0.343 — *below* 0.5, meaning the predictions
are anti-correlated with truth.

## Why the architecture is matched to the wrong problem on three of four regions

DEEP-SEAM's loss function looks for cells whose feature vectors
deviate from a Gaussian-distributed bulk. That assumption fits
Curnamona because the published preprocessing pipeline (isometric-
log-ratio transformation plus principal-components analysis of the
geochemistry, gray-level co-occurrence matrix textural features on
the geophysics) compresses the data into something that genuinely
approaches Gaussian, with the seven deposits sitting in the tails.

The same loss function fails on regional MPM because positive cells
there are not anomalies. They sit in *typical* geological contexts
that the proximity-to-black-shale, proximity-to-fault, and
sedimentary-dictionary features describe by design. A cell on a
carbonate platform near a black shale near a fault is a *good host*,
not an anomalous one. The signal in the data is "fits the geological
recipe," not "stands out from everything else." GBM can pick up the
recipe because it builds multivariate conditional rules. DevNet
looks for the rare and finds the recipe is *common*, so the deposits
do not appear anomalous and the model produces near-random ordering.

## Interesting finding 1: the architecture's advantage is dataset-specific, not algorithmic

We reproduced the original DEEP-SEAM result on Curnamona: DevNet beat
RF by a 2-3× factor at top-1 percent capture, matching the published
finding. The advantage did not transfer to any of the three other
regions:

- On **Tanacross**, RF beat DevNet 22 percent versus 13 percent at
  top-1 capture.
- On **Arizona**, 23 percent versus 9 percent.
- On the **Lawley continental MVT** dataset under spatially-blocked
  CV, the GBM landed 28 percent of held-out deposits in its top 1
  percent of cells while DevNet landed zero.

Three independent deposit types, three independent feature stacks,
one consistent direction of finding. The Curnamona result is real,
but it depends on the specific dataset construction (very small
positive set, heavy upstream PCA + GLCM, anomaly-detection framing)
the original authors built. Outside that construction the architecture
underperforms classical tree-based methods by 15 to 37 percentage
points of AUC.

## Interesting finding 2: the negative result is actionable methodological guidance for the field

The deep-anomaly architectures (DEEP-SEAM, DevNet, related
deviation-loss methods) are being proposed in the recent MPM
literature as the next step beyond classical tree-based machine
learning. The four-region result says, concretely, that they are
not yet ready as a drop-in replacement for Random Forest or gradient
boosting on standard regional prospectivity workflows. They have a
specific niche — small-positive datasets with heavy upstream feature
decomposition that genuinely shapes the data into anomaly form —
and outside that niche they lose by a wide margin.

For an exploration company, the practical implication is:

- For tightly-curated small-positive deposit types (kimberlites,
  carbonatite REE, unconformity uranium), the architecture may be a
  real win if the preprocessing pipeline can be reproduced on the
  target dataset.
- For broad-context regional MPM on standard public feature stacks
  (porphyry Cu, MVT Zn-Pb, orogenic gold, sediment-hosted Cu), the
  classical tree-based methods are still the right choice.

Knowing where the architecture works and where it does not is more
useful than a blanket claim that the new method is better.

## Files and reproduction

- Lawley Phase 3 (the head-to-head): [scripts/reproductions/lawley/phase3_devnet.py](https://github.com/00skyking00/ai-minerals/blob/main/scripts/reproductions/lawley/phase3_devnet.py)
- DevNet implementation: [src/ai_minerals/model_devnet.py](https://github.com/00skyking00/ai-minerals/blob/main/src/ai_minerals/model_devnet.py)

## Sources

- Pang, G. et al. 2019. Deep Anomaly Detection with Deviation Networks. arXiv:1911.08623.
- Luo, Z. et al. 2026. DEEP-SEAM: an explainable semi-supervised deep learning framework for mineral prospectivity mapping. *Geosci. Model Dev.* 19, 2593.
- Lawley, C. J. M. et al. 2022. Data-driven prospectivity modelling of sediment-hosted Zn-Pb mineral systems and their critical raw materials. *Ore Geology Reviews* 141, 104635.
